title: Farm Advisor/UC Cooperative Extension | Inyo County California
content: for Benefits Staff Water Department Documents Governing Documents Inyo/LA Water Agreement Green Book Stipulation & Orders Water Policy Water Purchase Policy MOU Groundwater Ordinance Drought Recovery Policy Land & Water Advisory Committees Hillside Decree Chandler Decree 1991 EIR LADWP Pumping Reports Bishop Cone Audit LADWP Annual OV Report Inyo County Water Dept Annual Report Inyo/LA Cooperative Studies Staff Reports & Journal Articles OV Water History OV Land Management Plan Blackrock 94 Maps & Data Hydrology Groundwater Monitoring Runoff and Pumping LADWP Data Vegetation Remote Sensing Rare Plants Well Management Historic Maps Baseline Vegetation Ritch Maps 1919 Bishop Maps Meetings Water Commission Members Inyo/LA Technical Group Inyo/LA Standing Cmte Projects Coso Hay Ranch Mitigation Lower Owens River Project Saltcedar SGMA Staff Farm Advisor/UC Cooperative Extension Home Services Farm Advisor/UC Cooperative Extension Carousel content A carousel is a rotating set of images, rotation stops on keyboard focus on carousel tab controls or hovering the mouse pointer over images. Use the tabs or the previous and next buttons to change the displayed slide. The Farm Advisor serves the people of Inyo and Mono counties in matters related to agricultural production by providing research-based production advisement. We also administer the local 4-H Youth Development Program, UC Master Gardeners, and the UC Master Food Preservers. The Farm Advisor conducts research and offers advisement in the following areas: Season extension Environmental Horticulture Pest control Best management practices for small-scale horticultural crops Food systems and market garden entrepreneurship Food safety practices for small operations Home food preservation Our goal is to help producers of all sizes fill in the gaps in knowledge that may hinder the viability of their enterprise. We can do this by cooperating on research projects, providing unbiased advice, and connecting you with other resources. If you are a producer of livestock, alfalfa, forages, or another crop and you need assistance, please contact us, as we can provide assistance through our statewide connections. We're your connection to the University of California's division of Agriculture and Natural Resources. In addition to advising food production, we can provide advisement in Environmental Horticulture issues. Nurseries, arborists, landscapers, and agencies should contact the Farm Advisor for support. Home gardeners who require assistance should contact our Master Gardener volunteers for answers to their gardening questions. The Community and Economic Development Advisor is available to assist community members in a number of areas: Public and professional education programs : Strengthening civic and leadership capacity through training on community and economic development topics. Data analysis and technical assistance materials : Providing economic profiles, policy reviews, and other data-driven resources to inform decision-making. Extension convening, planning, and development support : Facilitating strategic planning, stakeholder collaboration, and grant development for regional organizations. Communications resources : Enhancing regional connections through newsletters, social media updates, and real-time event promotion. Establishing connections to California's network of CED professionals : Leveraging statewide expertise and partnerships to support local development initiatives. Our Community Economic Development programming aims to create vibrant, resilient communities by supporting local businesses, fostering collaboration, and addressing critical needs like workforce development, small business growth, and entrepreneurship.

The Department provides administrative assistance to the Board of Supervisors. The Department develops and implements the County's Purchasing Program and Policy by providing training, guidance and support to County Departments in their purchasing needs. Address: Lassen County Administrative Services 221 S. Roop St., Ste. 4 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 am-5:00 pm Personnel & Risk Management The mission of Personnel & Risk Management Services is to provide productive use of personnel & risk services by attracting and retaining the most qualified individuals into public service; to ensure and promote quality customer service to County officials, departments, employees, and the public we serve, in compliance with federal, state and County ordinances and regulations. Personnel & Risk Management Services is a division of the Lassen County Administration Department. We hope you find our webpage useful and informative. Address: Lassen County Administration 221 S . Roop Street Suite 3 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Thursday: 7:30 am-5:30 pm Friday: 8:00 am-5:00 pm Agricultural Commissioner The Agricultural Commissioner promotes Lassen County agricultural production by protecting it from injurious pests and diseases, to ensure the safety and wholesomeness of food and other products for the consumer, and to build consumer and business confidence in the marketplace through the maintenance of equity. Assessor The duties of the county assessor are: to discover all assessable property, to inventory and list all taxable property, to value the property, and to enroll the property on the local assessment roll. The assessor’s primary responsibility is to annually determine the proper taxable value for each property so the owner is assured of paying the correct amount of property tax for the support of local government. Address: 107 S. Roop St. 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 9:00 am-12:00 pm , 1:00 pm-4:00 pm Auditor Address: 221 South Roop St. Ste. 1 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Board of Supervisors The Board of Supervisors is a five-member Board elected from five different supervisorial districts.

title: Departments | Lassen County
content: Reimbursement- Collections serves the public by providing assistance in paying court-ordered fines and fees. Collection Officers are professional and demonstrate sound communication in every financial assessment. Planning and Building Services Planning and Building Services is a multi-disciplinary department providing a range of planning and development services to citizens, appointed boards, and commissions. Our Department ensures that all development is consistent with State Law, Building Codes, the General Plan, Zoning Ordinance and Development Code. We work together to protect life and property, while improving quality of life and the environment. Address: 707 Nevada Street Ste 5 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 7:00 am-12:00 pm , 1:00 pm-4:00 pm Environmental Health We are pleased to announce that Environmental Health is now part of Planning and Building Services. Address: Main Office 707 Nevada St Ste. 5 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 am-4:00 pm Surveyor The Surveyor’s Office, a division of the Department of Planning and Building Services, provides certain services to the public and to other governmental agencies as requested. Please note that the Surveyor's Office does not offer private surveying services. See below for list of local engineers and surveyors. The Surveyor’s Office ensures that the review of Final Maps, Parcel Maps, Records of Survey, Lot Line Adjustments, Mergers, Certificates of Compliance, Notices of Violation, Corner Records and legal descriptions are in conformance with State and Local Ordinances and that the information provided on these documents is technically correct. The Surveyor’s Office is also responsible for maintaining Lassen County’s six digit addressing system. If you have further questions, please call the Surveyor Division, (530) 251-8269. Address: 707 Nevada Street Ste 5 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 7:00 am-12:00 pm , 1:00 pm-4:00 pm Probation Address: 2950 Riverside Dr, Suite 101 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 am-5:00 pm Juvenile Detention Facility Public Defender Thank you for visiting the Lassen County Public Defender Department. Please note this website is for informational purposes only. Always seek the advice of a qualified attorney for all legal matters. If you need to contact our department, please use the information provided in the contact area to the right. If you're viewing this page from your phone, simply scroll down to access our contact info. An appointment is required to meet with your attorney. Public Works/Roads The Roads Department provides funding for the maintenance of County road and bridge systems to safeguard the traveling public, and preserves the County's capital investment in the system. Airports The Airports Department is responsible for the administration, operation and maintenance of Lassen County's four airports located throughout the County. Address: 707 Nevada Street 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Animal Control Thank you for visiting the Lassen County Animal Control Department. content: Lassen County's four airports located throughout the County. Address: 707 Nevada Street 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Animal Control Thank you for visiting the Lassen County Animal Control Department. Address: Animal Shelter 472-000 Johnstonville Rd 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Buildings and Grounds The Roads Department provides funding for the maintenance of County road and bridge systems to safeguard the traveling public, and preserves the County's capital investment in the system. Cemeteries/Parks The Cemeteries Division is responsible for administration, maintenance and operation of Lassen County's cemetery sites which are located throughout the County. Address: 707 Nevada Street Suite 4 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Transportation The Lassen County Transportation Commission (LCTC) was formed in 1971. It is made up of three members of the Lassen County Board of Supervisors and three members of the Susanville City Council. Address: Executive Secretary Contact 1631 Alhambra Blvd., Suite 100 95816 Sacramento , CA See map: Google Maps Purchasing/Call for Bids Projects to Bid/ Requests for Proposals Sheriff's Office The Sheriff is responsible for a wide range of public safety services; including the prevention, detection and investigation of crimes in the unincorporated areas of the County, operating a 24 hour Adult Detention Facility (Jail), managing a 911 dispatch center, providing search and rescue services, providing boating safety services on navigable waters, providing court security services, serving all State mandated writs, warrants, and other notices issued by the Court, and serving as the Coroner of Lassen County. Address: Lassen County Sheriff's Office 1415 Sheriff Cady Ln 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Treasurer/Tax Collector The Treasurer/Tax Collector manages the investments of the County to ensure maximum interest income and adequate cash flow. This office also collects property tax revenue for county government, school districts and various special districts. Veterans Services The Lassen County Veterans Service Office provides assistance, counseling and acts as an advocate to the men and women who served in the Armed Forces of America, their dependents and survivors in obtaining benefits relative to federal, state, and local programs based on military service. Address: Memorial Building 1205 Main Street 96130 Susanville , CA See map: Google Maps Hours: Monday - Friday: 8:00 am-5:00 pm

title: Parks & Leisure | Lassen County
content: Skip to main content Search form Search County Select a Department Administration Personnel & Risk Management Agricultural Commissioner Assessor Auditor Board of Supervisors County Clerk - Recorder Elections Recorder County Clerk Clerk of the Board of Supervisors County Counsel Department of Child Support Services District Attorney Victim/Witness Assistance Mikailia Child Advocacy Fairgrounds Health and Social Services Behavioral Health Public Health Tobacco Use Reduction Program Housing and Grants Community Social Services Lassen Works Children & Family Services Adult Services Information Services Department Lassen Regional Solid Waste Management Authority Office of Emergency Services Office of Recovery and Reimbursement Planning and Building Services Environmental Health Surveyor Probation Juvenile Detention Facility Public Defender Public Works/Roads Animal Control Airports Cemeteries/Parks Transportation Buildings and Grounds Purchasing/Call for Bids Sheriff's Office Treasurer/Tax Collector Veterans Services Events History Lodging Parks & Leisure Recreation Restaurants Parks & Leisure Lassen Volcanic National Park Lassen Volcanic National Park is home to steaming fumaroles, meadows freckled with wildflowers, clear mountain lakes, and numerous volcanoes. Jagged peaks tell the story of its eruptive past while hot water continues to shape the land. Lassen Volcanic offers opportunities to discover the wonder and mysteries of volcanoes and hot water for visitors willing to explore the undiscovered. See also: Source: Lassen Volcanic National Park Lassen National Forest The Lassen National Forest lies at the heart of one of the most fascinating areas of California, called the Crossroads. Here the granite of the Sierra Nevada, the lava of the Cascades and the Modoc Plateau, and the sagebrush of the Great Basin meet and blend. Source: Lassen National Forest Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway Along this 500 mile journey from volcano to volcano you will find opportunities for adventure, exploration, communion with nature and an appreciation for the culture and history of the region. You will also find residents eager to share the beauty and mystery of this land that is dotted with evidence of an eruptive past. Source: Volcanic Legacy Scenic Byway Susanville Ranch Park Susanville Ranch Park is a 1,100-acre park property in northeast California, owned and managed by Lassen County and the Bureau of Land Management as a shared-use recreation area. Source: Susanville Ranch Park "Meadow in Lassen National Park" by Pierre Leclerc

title: EPIC-LA - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content EPIC-LA Home > EPIC-LA The County’s Electronic Permitting and Inspections portal, or EPIC-LA , is your site to submit applications, check on case status, and run reports. If you need technical help, please contact epiclahelp@lacounty.gov . Register You will need to register to submit applications, pay fees, and receive plans filed reports. How To Register How To Pay Online How To Add Another Contact To a Case Apply After registering and logging into EPIC-LA, you can submit a Base Application . This provides basic information to understand your project and convert your request to the appropriate case type. For example, your project may need a Site Plan Review or a Conditional Use Permit. Your Base Application RPAP number will be different than your RPPL plan number once accepted. How To Apply Online Search You can look up cases using the project number (e.g., PRJ2023-001234), plan number (e.g., RPPL2023001234), or location (e.g. partial address or Assessor’s Parcel Number). You can check status, contact the case planner, or check for payments due. You can also access related public documents. How to Look Up a Specific Case Generate Reports You can find out what cases were filed in your community or generate reports based on filed date and specific case types, such as Oak Tree Permits. More reports will be available soon! How To Generate Reports – Available Reports in EPIC-LA How To Receive Automated Reports – Available Automated Reports How to Look up and Follow Cases using Cases Filed Reports Note: Monthly Cases Filed reports are also available online. GeneraL question For general questions about planning, zoning, and application filing, please contact us. Please have the property’s Assessor’s Parcel Number (APN) or address ready. Phone Number: (213) 974-6411 Help Email: info@planning.lacounty.gov Hours of Operation: Monday through Thursday: 7:30am – 5:30pm Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Pay a Fee - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content Pay a fee Home > How Do I > Pay a Fee There are a few ways to pay a fee. APPLICATION FEE If you filed online, you will receive an email with your invoice number and EPIC-LA link to pay online after we convert your Base Application to the correct permit(s). Read the search case instructions and pay online instructions for more information. If you need more technical help, please contact epiclahelp@lacounty.gov . To pay in person, you can visit our field office locations during counter hours. Subdivision fees are only accepted online. To pay by mail, please make your check payable to “County of Los Angeles” and include the invoice number and RPPL case number. Send to: LA County Planning 320 W. Temple Street Los Angeles, CA 90012 DEPOSIT Some permits and applications have a draw-down account. With your first deposit, you will follow the same steps as paying an application fee. You will also need the Financial Responsibility form. Supplemental deposits follow the same payment steps as applications. If there is a change in ownership or financial responsibility, you will need to submit a Change of Financial Responsibility form . If you send these forms by mail, by email, or by someone who is not the responsible party, the forms have to be notarized. NONCOMPLIANCE FEE If you received a Second Notice of Noncompliance Fee letter, it is important to work with the Investigating Planner listed in the letter to make sure the fee is paid on time and avoid additional penalty fees. Visit Zoning Enforcement How to Pay a Noncompliance Fee for more information. Visit our fee schedule for more information. Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Check Status of an Application - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content CHECK STATUS OF AN APPLICATION Home > How Do I > Check Status of an Application You can use EPIC-LA to check any application status. You can also look up the case planner, check for payments due, or access related public documents. You will need some information to start. PROJECT In EPIC-LA, a project is a related group of permits or plans. The project number starts with a year, and then six numbers. Example: 2020-001234. PLAN In EPIC-LA, a plan is a type of application. We use “plan” in EPIC-LA, but other Los Angeles County departments, like Public Works, use “permit.” Our plan numbers start with four letters: RPAP, for a base application, or RPPL, for an accepted application. After the letters are 10 numbers, including the year. Example: RPPL2020001234. LOCATION In EPIC-LA, you can use part of an address or the Assessor’s Parcel Number. The APN is also known as Assessor’s Identification Number, or AIN. This is a 10-digit number assigned by the LA County Assessor . Read the case look-up instructions for more information. Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Climate Action and Sustainability - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content Climate Action & Sustainability Home > Climate Action and Sustainability Planning for climate change is a priority for LA County Planning. In response to the changing climate and urgency for action, we have many projects and policies that address climate adaptation and resiliency. Our work serves communities through broad climate adaptation policies in the General Plan to focused ordinances on natural resource protection to preserve the County’s unique biodiversity. Our commitment to the stewardship of the environment will also benefit the communities of LA County. All Climate Action and Sustainability Climate Action Plan Read More → Community Wildfire Protection Ordinance Read More → Conservation and Natural Resources Element Read More → Countywide Community Wildfire Protection Plan Read More → Green Zones Program Read More → Significant Ecological Areas Program Read More → Technical Update Read More → Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Coastal Planning - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content Coastal Planning Home > Coastal Planning The California Coastal Zone is about 840 miles of coastline and about 287 miles of shoreline around 9 offshore islands. The Coastal Zone extends 3 miles into the ocean. Inland, the Coastal Zone can vary from a few hundred feet up to 5 miles. The 1976 California Coastal Act mapped the Coastal Zone. The Coastal Act also established a comprehensive coastal protection program. The state Coastal Commission became responsible for coastal resources protection. Some Coastal Zone issues include shoreline public access, visitor accommodations, sensitive habitats, and visual resources. Commercial fisheries and water quality are also some of the issues addressed. County Coastal Areas Five coastal areas fall within LA County Planning: Marina del Rey Santa Catalina Island Santa Monica Mountains San Clemente Island Ballona Wetlands Area A We have certified Local Coastal Programs for Marina del Rey, Santa Catalina Island, and the Santa Monica Mountains. San Clemente Island is the southernmost of the California Channel Islands and has been owned and operated by the United States Navy since 1934. Ballona Wetlands Area A is an ecological reserve owned by the State of California and managed by the State Department of Fish and Wildlife. Local Coastal Program A Local Coastal Program is the main land use document for coastal area development and natural resource protection. Each LCP has: A Land Use Plan. This designates goals and policies, land use classifications, and type and density of allowable development. A Local Implementation Plan. This identifies specific zoning regulations and procedures for development. The Coastal Commission certifies LCPs, and then we have authority to issue permits. All Coastal Planning Marina del Rey Land Use Plan Read More → Pepperdine University Long Range Development Plan Read More → Santa Catalina Island Local Coastal Plan Read More → Santa Monica Mountains Local Coastal Program Read More → Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Connect With A Planner - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content Connect With A Planner Home > Connect With A Planner We are not offering virtual counseling appointments related to disaster recovery at this time.  For assistance related to disaster recovery, please visit a One-Stop Permit Center . Virtual Counseling Connect with a planner through virtual counseling. There are three options for any property in unincorporated LA County. For properties in Hacienda Heights, Rowland Heights or Lake Los Angeles, an additional counseling option is available below under Other: Zoning and Planning Discussion Choose this option if you have draft development plans or project-specific questions for sites in a special area. Special areas include a Significant Ecological Area, hillsides, and Community Standards District. This is also a good option if you need early counseling on more complicated projects. Case Intake / Online Application Help Choose this option if you need help submitting an application through our online permitting and inspections portal, EPIC-LA . You will need to be able to share your screen for this option. Zoning Enforcement Violation Discussion Choose this option if Zoning Enforcement referred you to discuss abating your violation. You’ll need to provide your violation case number and detailed list of alleged violation(s). Schedule an Appointment Once you know the virtual counseling type: Schedule a Virtual Counseling Appointment Other In-Person or Virtual One-Stop Counseling (Hacienda Heights, Rowland Heights, or Lake Los Angeles) Schedule a One-Stop Counseling Appointment Choose In-Person or Virtual One-Stop Counseling if you are within Hacienda Heights, Rowland Heights, or Lake Los Angeles and wish to discuss your development project, need help with the application process, or need guidance with how to address a zoning violation. Appointments are currently available in-person in our Field Offices (La Puente Field Office for Hacienda Heights and Rowland Heights, Antelope Valley Field Office for Lake Los Angeles). Virtual meetings are also an option. For further information, please review our flyers for services in Hacienda Heights, Rowland Heights, and Lake Los Angeles. Subdivision Virtual Counseling Schedule a Subdivision Virtual Counseling Appointment Choose Subdivisions Virtual Counseling if you have general questions regarding the subdivision process, SB 9 and SB 684 Urban Lot Splits, Condominiums, Lot Line Adjustments, Certificates of Compliance and all things related to the creation of new lots. Counseling meetings are available Monday through Friday, from 10:00 am to 10:30 am.  For more detailed-specific counseling we highly recommend a Pre-Application Counseling meeting . We also provide other types of counseling for permits planning. If you need other help, email us at info@planning.lacounty.gov . Scroll to Top Skip to content

title: Epic La How To Lookup Specific Case
content:
HOW TO LOOK UP A SPECIFIC CASE: 

If you already know the address or case number of a project, you can look up the status, planner 
assigned, and other case details using the EPIC-LA site. 

1. Click on the Search tab at the top. Then, select Plan under the search bar and type in the case 
number that you are interested in (should start with “RPPL” or “RPAP”) and select Search. 

2. Click on the case number to pull up additional details on the case: 

3. From this screen, you can see the case status, planner contact information, and additional case 

information: 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Tip: You can click on the case planner’s name to send an email requesting to be added to the hearing 
notification list for the case or to submit a public comment.

title: RPC History - LA County Planning
content: Skip to content LA County Planning Home > RPC History RPC History We recently celebrated 100 years of the Los Angeles County Regional Planning Commission . In late 1922, the County Board of Supervisors created the Commission with staff to coordinate land use planning within LA County . The first regional planning commissioners were appointed by the Board in early 1923. This makes the RPC the oldest county planning commission in the United States. We are proud and humbled in recognizing the role planning has played in LA County over the past 100 years. Anniversary Brochure Browse through our commemorative brochure and follow the changes to our Commission and the region through the decades. STORY MAP Explore 100 years of the Los Angeles County Regional Planning Commission through our StoryMap ! Board Presentation Supervisor Kathryn Barger celebrated the Los Angeles County Regional Planning Commission and 100 years of service. Scroll to Top Skip to content

Join Mariposa County in celebrating the United States of America's 250th Birthday. Living Here As the home of Yosemite National Park, Mariposa County offers stunning natural beauty and world-class recreational opportunities. With no incorporated cities (or stop lights), the County's 17,000 residents enjoy the slow pace of life. History Nestled in the Sierra Nevada mountains, Mariposa County is rich in history, culture and natural beauty. From early native people to gold-seeking settlers to today’s tourists, inhabitants and visitors have shaped a legacy of discovery and transformation. Things to Do There’s so much to do in Mariposa County! The County offers endless opportunities for outdoor recreation, including hiking, biking, rafting, and scenic exploration, along with historic downtown charm and rich cultural experiences. See More Stories /CivicAlerts.aspx WHAT TO KNOW NOW…

title: Doing Business | Mariposa County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Doing Business Doing Business Why Start a Business in Mariposa County Mariposa County offers a unique environment for business development, combining a business-friendly regulatory framework with the charm and advantages of a rural setting. Entrepreneurs are drawn to the County because of the absence of business license requirements, a supportive community, and access to abundant natural beauty—making it an attractive destination for both traditional businesses and innovative ventures. The county’s strategic location near Yosemite National Park also presents unique opportunities for tourism-driven businesses such as hospitality, retail, and outdoor recreation services. Mariposa County's commitment to supporting agriculture and rural industries fosters an environment rich with opportunities for innovation and growth. Our Industries Despite its small size, Mariposa County is home to a diverse range of industries including: Aerospace Agriculture Construction Education Forestry Healthcare Mining Real Estate & Property Management Retail Tourism & Hospitality Wine Mariposa Countywide Nonresidential Market Study To support sustainable economic growth in the area, Mariposa County conducted a Nonresidential Market Study to evaluate the economic health of local industries and identify opportunities for enhancing nonresidential land use. This study aligns with the County’s 2017 Economic Vitality Strategy detailed below. Key components include a retail demand analysis, identification of gaps in commercial services, and strategies designed to foster a balanced and thriving economy for residents, businesses, and visitors. Read the study: https://www.mariposacounty.org/DocumentCenter/View/96212/Mariposa-County-Market-Study [WF1] The Mariposa County Economic Vitality Strategy (EVS) The Mariposa County Economic Vitality Strategy (EVS), first developed in 2007, aims to strengthen tourism and diversify the local economy while preserving the County’s values and quality of life. In 2017, the County published an updated study that further focuses on refining economic development approaches, streamlining regulations, and aligning land use with current needs to ensure a prosperous future for the County. Read the study: https://www.mariposacounty.org/DocumentCenter/View/57645/Mariposa-County-EVS_Final-Report-62017?bidId= Mariposa County Economic & Demographic Profile The economic and demographic profile of Mariposa County is outlined in the following 2018 report published by the Rural County Representatives of California (RCRC): https://www.mariposacounty.org/DocumentCenter/View/79698/Mariposa-County-Economic-and-Demographic-Profile?bidId= Local Business Support Organizations Mariposa County Chamber of Commerce provides support to local businesses through advocacy, networking, and promotional opportunities. Learn more at https://www.mariposachamber.org . Made in Mariposa (MiM) is a nonprofit organization dedicated to supporting artists, crafters, and local producers in Mariposa County. Operated as a cooperative of artisans, the MiM store provides a shared space where members can showcase and sell their handmade goods, fostering creativity and community. https://madeinmariposa.org Motherload Job Training offers a range of services designed to support businesses in the region. They provide strategies to enhance hiring and retention, helping companies find and keep qualified employees. In addition, the organization connects businesses to a network of community resources and agencies, strengthening the local workforce and driving economic growth. https://www.mljt.org UCMerced Small Business Development Center (SBDC) fosters the development, growth and success of small businesses and aspiring entrepreneurs throughout Merced and Mariposa Counties to enhance individual, family, community and regional prosperity. They offer no-cost, personalized, confidential one-on-one advice for start-ups, entrepreneurs and existing businesses. https://ucmercedsbdc.com Broadband Businesses in the downtown Mariposa commercial district have

title: Public Works - Parks and Recreation Divison | Mariposa County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Government Public Works Parks and Recreation Public Works - Parks and Recreation Divison Overview The Mariposa County Public Works Parks and Recreation Division manages recreation activities in County facilities and assists the public with making reservations in those facilities. Mariposa County Parks and Recreation Division currently employs one Parks and Recreation Manager, a Recreation Supervisor, and 30 Extra Help employees. The Parks and Recreation Division offers a variety of valuable services to the community throughout the year. Call our office for more information on current programs or check out our Facebook page . Contact Us Parks & Recreation Physical Address 4998 County Park Road P.O. title: Behavioral Health & Recovery Services | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Services Departments & Services A-G Behavioral Health & Recovery Services Behavioral Health & Recovery Services Overview Merced County Behavioral Health and Recovery Services (BHRS) provides an array of behavioral health and supportive services to the population of Merced County, living with a Serious Mental Illness or Substance Use Disorders. BHRS provides a seamless system of care for our consumers through a collaborative network of County operated programs, County contracted agencies, and other community resources. Services are available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year and can be accessed by appointment, walk-in, or by contacting BHRS crisis and emergency services. The Behavioral Health Advisory Board (BHAB) is established, per WIC 5604, to review the community’s mental health needs and services, and make recommendations to the BHRS Director. The BHAB is comprised of 16-19 members, including Supervisor Pareira and representatives from each District. Mission Statement Behavioral Health and Recovery Services is committed to empowering our diverse community with hope, recovery and wellness by providing comprehensive holistic care. Vision Statement Inspiring hope and recovery for those we serve as the premier provider for quality whole person care. Core Values Humility  ●  Integrity  ●  Compassion  ●  Innovation  ●  Customer Service  ●  Inclusion Who We Serve Prevention and Crisis Services: ALL county residents regardless of insurance. Focus Population BHRS provides treatment and intervention services primarily for individuals on Medi-Cal or without insurance who: Are severely mentally ill Have substance use disorder Merced County Population: 290,014 Total eligible Medi-Cal population: 156,219 or 53.8% of the county population Total served by BHRS: 5,284 (or Penetration Rate of 3.38% ); Medium size counties: 3.49% ; State 3.96% Youth 0-17: 1,666 Adults 18-64: 3,412 Older Adults  65+: 206 *CY 2022 data 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline If you or someone you know needs support now, call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org CredibleMind CredibleMind is a free online platform that provides 24/7 confidential mental health and well-being resources. The CredibleMind platform covers over 100 topics, spanning from: stress, anxiety, depression, relationships, mindfulness, coping with medical diagnosis, happiness, parenting, grief and loss, substance use, and aging. The platform allows users to maintain anonymity and track their improvement through assessments. Kimiko Vang, DSW, LCSW BHRS Director / Public Guardian / Conservator Contact Us Physical Address: 301 East 13th Street Merced, California 95341 Phone: (209) 381-6800 Toll Free: (888) 334-0163 TDD Toll-Free: (866) 293-1818 [email protected] Directory View All FAQs /FAQ.aspx Quick Links Credible Mind Merced County Government Websites by CivicPlus®

title: Concealed Carry Weapons (CCW) | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Services Departments & Services H-Z Sheriff's Office Information Concealed Carry Weapons (CCW) Concealed Carry Weapons (CCW) Online Application System The Merced County Sheriff’s Office has streamlined the Concealed Carry Permit (CCW) process by allowing both new and renewal applicants to fill out an application online. With the online system, permits can be applied for any time, day or night, weekends or holidays at the comfort of your home. The Sheriff’s Office CCW Bureau strives to improve the way we help the public and want to make the CCW process as easy as possible. To apply online, residents should visit Permitium or click the Permitium tab below. A link to the secure online application system will appear, allowing applicants to create an account and submit information for a background check, and schedule appointments with our CCW Bureau. Residents can choose to receive notifications by email, text message, or phone call. A valid driver’s license or state ID card verifying residency is required along with two other forms of proof of residency in Merced County. New CCW applicants can apply prior to completing their 16-hour firearms training course. Applicants who wish to renew an existing permit must create an online account with the new system and follow all the prompts. Renewal permits require proof of residency along with their 8-hour firearms training course certificate submitted within their CCW Renewal Application. For assistance or information, please contact the CCW Bureau Office at (209) 385-7559 or [email protected] . 9 1 2 2 CCW Approved Instructor Classes Frequently Asked Questions Non-Resident CCW Information Government Websites by CivicPlus®

title: California SB 1383 - Organic Waste | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Services Departments & Services H-Z Public Works SB 1383 - Organic Waste California SB 1383 - Organic Waste What is Senate Bill 1383? SB 1383 , signed into law in 2016, mandates a statewide reduction in organic waste disposal. The goal is to cut organic waste in landfills by 75% by 2025, compared to 2014 levels. SB 1383 is the most significant waste reduction mandate in the last 30 years and expands upon the State’s initiatives to reduce emissions of greenhouse gasses (GHG) and air pollution statewide. Methane emissions resulting from the decomposition of organic waste in landfills are a significant source of GHG emissions contributing to global climate change. The law grants the California Department of Resources Recycling and Recovery (CalRecycle) the regulatory authority required to achieve the organic waste disposal reduction targets statewide and requires California to increase edible food recovery by 20% by 2025. Food waste alone accounts for approximately 17-18% of total landfill disposal. Increasing food waste prevention and encouraging edible food rescue, will help reduce methane emissions from organic waste disposed of in California's landfills. The new regulations require significant changes in the County's recycling and garbage collection, edible food recovery, organic material procurement, and record-keeping. The County is seeking to reduce impact to the customers by applying for certain waivers; however, these waivers are approved at the discretion of CalRecycle. Franchise Agreements AB2048 was signed into law on September 22, 2022, requiring jurisdictions to post on its website all current franchise agreements between contract solid waste and recycling haulers and the jurisdiction. Waste Management - Contract Gilton Solid Waste Management, Inc. - Contract Mid Valley Disposal - Contract What Does This Mean for Me? As a resident of Merced County, you may experience new mandatory garbage and recycling collection services. Unfortunately, these changes will result in an increase in monthly service fees. Businesses will also need to comply with the same requirements for recycling both organic and inorganic materials. Additionally, commercial food generators will now be required to donate any edible food that would otherwise be thrown away. In accordance with Prop 218, an Informational Mailer ( Aviso Informativo / Ceeb Toom Tshaj Tawm ) is to be dispatched to residents of Merced County with information about the changes to come, as well as some important dates: Tuesday, January 28, 2025 – 10:00 AM — Public Hearing - Recording Thursday, December 5, 2024 – 6:00 PM — Virtual Community Meeting - Recording Residential Commercial Commercial Waiver Information FAQs Cart Rates Who Is My Hauler Edible Food Recovery and Generator Information Residential Gilton Solid Waste Beginning July 1, 2025 , Gilton Solid Waste residential customers in affected census tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 2-cart system. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Black 90 gallon Household trash, glass bottles & containers, plastic bottles & containers, cans tin Weekly Green 90 gallon Wood waste, green materials, fiber (paper & cardboard), food scraps and soiled paper Weekly Waste

The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Black 96 gallon Foam containers, garden hose, broken ceramic, candy & snack wrappers, diapers Weekly Green 96 gallon Food waste, yard waste Weekly Blue 96 gallon Plastic bottles, food & beverage cans, glass bottles & containers, food & beverage cartons Weekly Mid Valley Disposal Beginning July 1, 2025 , Mid Valley Disposal residential customers in affected census tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 3-cart system. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Gray 96 gallon Styrofoam, clothes, pet waste, carpet, toys, palm fronds, shoes, toilet paper, plastic bags Weekly Blue 96 gallon Mixed paper, cardboard, aluminum & tin, cans, glass bottles & containers Weekly Green 96 gallon Tree trimmings, garden clippings, untreated wood, grass, food soiled paper, soiled cardboard, fruits, vegetables, eggshells, nutshell, cooked meat/poultry/fish Weekly Commercial Gilton Solid Waste Beginning July 1, 2025 , Gilton Solid Waste commercial customers in affected census tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 1-cart system, with the option of a second cart if a high level of organic waste is generated. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Bin/Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Black (Sizes vary ) Household trash, glass bottles & containers, plastic bottles & containers, cans tin Weekly Green (Optional) 90 gallon Wood waste, green materials, fiber (paper & cardboard), food scraps and soiled paper Weekly Waste Management Beginning July 1, 2025 , Waste Management commercial customers in affected census tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 3- cart system. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Bin/Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Black (Sizes vary) Foam containers, garden hose, broken ceramic, candy & snack wrappers, diapers Weekly Green 96 gallon Food waste, yard waste Weekly Blue (Sizes vary) Plastic bottles, food & beverage cans, glass bottles & containers, food & beverage cartons Weekly Mid Valley Disposal Beginning July 1, 2025 , Mid Valley Disposal commercial customers in affected census tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 3-cart system. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Bin/Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Gray (Sizes vary ) Styrofoam, clothes, pet waste, carpet, toys, palm fronds, shoes, toilet paper, plastic bags Weekly Green (Sizes vary) Tree trimmings, garden clippings, untreated wood, grass, food soiled paper, soiled cardboard, fruits,

title: California SB 1383 - Organic Waste | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: tracts will be required to source-separate their waste using a 3-cart system. The new standard of collection will consist of: Cart Color Bin/Cart Size Examples of Approved Contents Collection Frequency Gray (Sizes vary ) Styrofoam, clothes, pet waste, carpet, toys, palm fronds, shoes, toilet paper, plastic bags Weekly Green (Sizes vary) Tree trimmings, garden clippings, untreated wood, grass, food soiled paper, soiled cardboard, fruits, vegetables, eggshells, nutshells, cooked meat / poultry / fish Blue (Sizes vary) Mixed paper, cardboard, aluminum & tin, cans, glass bottles & containers Weekly Commercial Waiver Information Commercial Waiver Information Organics Recycling Waiver Request Merced County Code Chapter 9.06.100 FAQs SB 1383 FAQs for Residents and Businesses What is SB 1383? SB 1383 is a California law aimed at reducing organic waste in landfills by 75% by 2025. It focuses on enhancing composting and recycling programs to help combat climate change. Who is affected by SB 1383? Both residents and businesses in Merced County are affected. This includes households, commercial food generators, and any businesses generating organic waste. What changes should residents expect? Residents will see new mandatory garbage and recycling collection services, including separate collection for organic waste. This may result in an increase in monthly service costs. What are organic materials? Organic materials include food scraps, yard waste, and other biodegradable items. These should be composted or recycled rather than disposed of in regular trash. What are the requirements for businesses? Businesses must implement organic and inorganic recycling practices. Additionally, commercial food generators are now required to donate any edible food that would otherwise go to waste. How can residents participate? Residents should separate organic waste from general trash, use compost bins, and participate in local recycling programs. Educational resources will be available to help guide this process. Where can I find more information? For more details, residents and businesses can contact their local waste management authority or Merced County Public Works Department: Gilton - Customer Service - Gilton Solid Waste Management Mid Valley - Contact - Mid Valley Disposal Waste Management - Customer Support | Waste Management Merced County Public Works Department – [email protected] How can I stay informed about new programs? Stay tuned for communications from your local waste management authority, including newsletters, community meetings, and online resources that will provide updates and information on SB 1383. What benefits will come from these changes? By participating in these programs, residents and businesses will help reduce greenhouse gas emissions, conserve resources, and contribute to a cleaner, healthier environment for the community. Can I throw plastic bags into the carts? Plastic bags are only acceptable in landfill carts. Please do not use any bags in the organics or recycling carts. Bags labeled “compostable” or “biodegradable” are also not accepted. What can I put in the different colored carts? Each cart color represents a specific type of waste. To find out what items are acceptable for each hauler, please use the appropriate links below: Gilton Sorting Guide Waste Management - Residential Sorting Guide Waste Management - Commercial Sorting Guide Mid Valley Disposal Sorting Guide What items

title: California SB 1383 - Organic Waste | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: labeled “compostable” or “biodegradable” are also not accepted. What can I put in the different colored carts? Each cart color represents a specific type of waste. To find out what items are acceptable for each hauler, please use the appropriate links below: Gilton Sorting Guide Waste Management - Residential Sorting Guide Waste Management - Commercial Sorting Guide Mid Valley Disposal Sorting Guide What items are NOT allowed in the carts? Hazardous items are prohibited in all carts. These include computers, phones, printers, TVs, monitors, fluorescent light bulbs, sharps or syringes, e-cigarettes, batteries, pesticides, propane tanks, paint, medications, lighters, automotive oil and filters, solvents and acids, pool chemicals, and more. Each cart also has its own set of unacceptable items. For more information, refer to the links provided: Gilton Sorting Guide Waste Management - Residential Sorting Guide Waste Management - Commercial Sorting Guide Mid Valley Disposal Sorting Guide What should I do if my cart is damaged? Gilton – Gilton offers onsite lid or wheel replacements for residential customers and will exchange carts with holes. Contact Gilton Customer Service for details. Waste Management – To request a container repair or replacement, visit Modesto Garbage Co., Inc. - Service Questions . Mid Valley – Customers are entitled to one free replacement per year for normal wear and tear (broken lids, damaged wheels, etc.). If damage is due to negligence (e.g., fire or hazardous waste disposal), a replacement fee will apply. Please call Mid Valley Disposal for rates. What happens if my cart is “contaminated”? Gilton – If your cart is tagged for contamination and hasn't been serviced, please remove all contaminants and visit the Gilton Customer Service website or contact them to get your service restored. Waste Management – TBA Mid Valley – If a driver tags your container for contamination, a recycling coordinator will send educational materials to help you understand proper recycling practices. How do I know if I am in a waiver zone? Visit SB 1383 Map Application: Waiver Identifier | Merced County RWA ExB and input your service address to find out if your service address applies to census tracks that have a population density of less than 75 people per square mile that are served by the County and are located in the unincorporated portion of the county. How can I apply for a low population waiver? Merced County has already identified and applied for waivers in census tracts with a population density of less than 75 people per square mile, located in unincorporated areas of the County. These areas are listed on the SB 1383 Map Application: Waiver Identifier | Merced County RWA ExB . Additionally, customers may qualify for either a De Minimis waiver or a Space Constraint waiver. Are there additional waivers? De Minimis Waivers: The County may waive certain Recyclable Materials and Organic Materials requirements for Commercial Businesses if they provide documentation (or if the County has evidence) demonstrating one of the following conditions: If the total Solid Waste Collection service is two (2) cubic yards or more per week, and Organic Materials make up

title: California SB 1383 - Organic Waste | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: waiver or a Space Constraint waiver. Are there additional waivers? De Minimis Waivers: The County may waive certain Recyclable Materials and Organic Materials requirements for Commercial Businesses if they provide documentation (or if the County has evidence) demonstrating one of the following conditions: If the total Solid Waste Collection service is two (2) cubic yards or more per week, and Organic Materials make up less than 20 gallons per week (per applicable Container) of the total waste; or, If the total Solid Waste Collection service is less than two (2) cubic yards per week, and Organic Materials make up less than 10 gallons per week (per applicable Container) of the total waste. Space Constraint Waiver: The County may waive certain Recyclable Materials or Organic Materials Collection requirements if the Commercial Business can demonstrate, through documentation or evidence from the County or hauler, that there is insufficient space to accommodate all necessary Containers. How can I protest the proposed rate increase? The County will accept written protests against the proposed rate increase from property owners and occupants subject to mandatory service. (PLEASE NOTE: emails or faxes will NOT be accepted). Objections to the rate increase can be submitted at the public hearing until the end of the public input portion of the hearing. They can also be mailed but must be received before the Public Hearing or hand-delivered to the County Clerk of the Board office located on the 3rd floor of the County’s Main Administration Building (2222 M Street) at or before the Public Hearing. If the majority (50%+1) of property owners and occupants subject to mandatory service submit valid written protests, the proposed rate increase will not be implemented. Valid Written Protests Must Include: Property owner/occupant name Property owner/occupant signature Service address or parcel number A statement that you are protesting the proposed rates Cart Rates Cart Rates by Hauler Gilton Solid Waste - Cart Rates Waste Management - Cart Rates Mid Valley Disposal - Cart Rates Who Is My Hauler Find Your Hauler or Zone Area To find your zone or to view the haulers in Merced County, use the Hauler / Zone Interactive Web Map . Edible Food Recovery and Generator Information Fighting Climate Change by Recycling Organic Waste In September 2016, Governor Edmund Brown Jr. set methane emissions reduction targets for California (SB 1383 Lara, Chapter 395, Statutes of 2016) in a statewide effort to reduce emissions of short-lived climate pollutants (SLCP). The targets must: Reduce organic waste disposal 75% by 2025. Rescue for people to eat at least 20% of currently disposed surplus food by 2025. Landfills Are Third Largest Source of Methane in California Organic waste in landfills emits: 20% of the state’s methane, a climate super pollutant 84 times more potent than carbon dioxide. Air pollutants like PM 2.5, which contributes to health conditions like asthma. Organics like food scraps, yard trimmings, paper, and cardboard make up half of what Californians dump in landfills. Reducing Short-Lived Climate Super Pollutants like organic waste will have the fastest impact on the climate crisis. Food Recovery Organizations and

title: California SB 1383 - Organic Waste | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: state’s methane, a climate super pollutant 84 times more potent than carbon dioxide. Air pollutants like PM 2.5, which contributes to health conditions like asthma. Organics like food scraps, yard trimmings, paper, and cardboard make up half of what Californians dump in landfills. Reducing Short-Lived Climate Super Pollutants like organic waste will have the fastest impact on the climate crisis. Food Recovery Organizations and Food Recovery Services Operating in Merced County SB1383 has categorized all businesses that generate organic waste as Tier 1 or Tier 2 Organic Waste Generators. Tier 1 and Tier 2 Organic waste generators are now required to contract with a local Food Recovery Organization or Food Recovery Service in order to donate any edible donatable food that would be thrown away. Food Recovery Organizations with the help of Food Recovery Services collect the edible donatable food and redistribute it to the public. title: Jail Information | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: Skip to Main Content Home Services Departments & Services H-Z Sheriff's Office Sheriff's Office Jail Information Jail Information Court Information Merced County courts hours of operation are Monday through Friday, 8:00 am to 5:00 pm. For court information, please call (209) 725-4100 . Visitation Information For information on visiting hours for the Main Jail and John Latorraca Correctional Center check out the Jail Visiting Schedule . For information on visitation access, view Policy 10.01 Visitation Access to Inmates . What Program The Jails Provide Browse the following tabs below for further information What To Expect Employment Groups Release & Transition What To Expect Upon entering the program facility, each participant can expect these events: Individual assessment and treatment planning Weekly cogitative behavioral therapy meetings 6-10 hours of cognitive behavioral groups per week (i.e. employment readiness, cognitive skill, substance abuse, etc.) Referrals to community resources Periodic evaluation of progress Awards and affirmation for compliance Employment The Reentry program staff will assist participants in job preparation and placement services upon release, however, it is the participant’s responsibility to pursue any job leads. Although it is difficult to find employment due to criminal background, it is the expectation of the program that you inform any prospective employer of your criminal history. This facility is committed to locating employers that will hire ex-offenders in order to provide stable employment situations. Groups An individual treatment plan and schedule will be developed for the inmate, which will address the specific program needs. The purpose of this plan will be to focus on the areas of an individual’s life that are most likely to lead him or her back to a criminal lifestyle. This schedule includes up to 6 to 10 hours of classes per week. All participants are assigned to an MRT® group. All additional groups will be assigned based on an individual’s needs. Classes are offered throughout the day to accommodate various schedules. All assigned groups require mandatory attendance and participation. Failure to attend groups as scheduled or failure to participate while in group will prevent the participant from progressing through the Program and can lead to unsuccessful discharge. Groups Available Through the Day Reporting Program Cognitive Skills: Moral Reconation Therapy (MRT) Substance Abuse Education Services Employment Readiness/ Vocational Job Searching Anger Management Life Skills Women’s Group Release & Transition Each participant will develop a Release Plan 30 to 60 days priors to the expected release. The Release Plan is a form filled out to address needs prior to and during reentry. Each participant will also complete a Transition Plan to address needs to be met in the community. Reentry Program staff will work with each participant in developing an individualized Transition Plan to show a way forward for recovery-oriented and crime-free lifestyle in the community. A passport to Action will be completed along with each Transition Plan. Transition Plans may include continued programming at the Merced County Day Reporting Center (DRC) along with referrals to community resources. Referrals may include living arrangements, a plan to secure Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) or Narcotics Anonymous (NA) sponsorship, involvement from

title: Jail Information | Merced County, CA - Official Website
content: to show a way forward for recovery-oriented and crime-free lifestyle in the community. A passport to Action will be completed along with each Transition Plan. Transition Plans may include continued programming at the Merced County Day Reporting Center (DRC) along with referrals to community resources. Referrals may include living arrangements, a plan to secure Alcoholics Anonymous (AA) or Narcotics Anonymous (NA) sponsorship, involvement from family/significant others, outpatient counseling arrangements, a plan for relapse prevention and crisis management, and specific plans for education and/or employment. Program Details Behavior Intervention 12 Step Program, Alcoholics Anonymous, Narcotics Anonymous, Anger Management, Parenting, Life Skills, Job Placement General Education Development Inmates are able to obtain their General Education Development and are able to challenge the G.E.D. test at any time. Celebrate Recovery Inmates are able to enroll in Celebrate Recovery through the Chaplin Services. Elements Completion Requirements Phase 1 - Orientation, Behavior Change Plan, Weekly Groups, Assessments, 30 minute Weekly Sessions Completion of all orientation elements and Step 3 of MRT Phase 2 - 30 minute Weekly Individual Sessions, Weekly Groups (MRT, Anger Management, etc.) Progress on Behavior Change Plan, Completion of Step 3 of MRT and Sobriety no less than 60 days. Phase 3 - 30 minute Weekly Individual Sessions, Weekly Groups (MRT, Anger Management, etc.), Release Plan and Transition Plan in place Completion of Behavior Change Plan goals, Completion of Step 12 of MRT, Release Plan and Transition Plan in place, Sobriety no less than 90 days. title: Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council | Mono County California
content: Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council | Mono County California Skip to main content Mono County California’s Eastern Sierra Search form Search Child Abuse Prevention Council (CAPC) Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council Mission The Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council (CAPC) is dedicated to supporting activities, resources, and programs that encourage safe, healthy, and resilient families. Join the Council CAPC brings together community members and local agencies to promote child safety, support family wellbeing, and strengthen prevention efforts across Mono County. Council members participate in quarterly public meetings, review local prevention priorities, and support outreach and awareness efforts such as Child Abuse Prevention Month activities. Apply Now Council Meetings Next Meeting: June 11, 2026 at 10:30 AM Location: Mono County Office of Education–South, Conference Room 451 Sierra Park Rd Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546 The February 2026 meeting was canceled. Any additional meetings scheduled prior to June will be posted here. Agenda and packet materials will be posted prior to each meeting. Meetings are open to the public and include time for public comment. For accessibility accommodations, contact klian@monocoe.org at least 48 hours in advance. Mono County Community Resource Guide The Mono County Community Resource Guide is published by the Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council in partnership with the Mono County Office of Education. The guide includes hotlines, child care, housing, health, education, and other local and statewide resources for families and service providers. Download PDF Open Excel Version Submit Edits or Updates About the Council Established in 2001, the Mono County Child Abuse Prevention Council supports coordination, education, and prevention efforts that strengthen families and reduce child abuse and neglect. Recognizing the rural and geographically isolated nature of Mono County, the council works to identify local needs, review programs funded through the Office of Child Abuse Prevention, and strengthen collaboration among family-serving organizations. The Mono County Department of Health and Human Services contracts with the Mono County Office of Education to coordinate CAPC activities. Members DonnaLisa Knowles – Parent & Community Member, Realtor Molly DesBaillets – First 5 Mono County Leslie Gaunt – Mono County Health and Human Services Jazmin Barkley – Mono County Probation Department The Child Abuse Prevention Council includes representatives from local agencies, service providers, and community members committed to strengthening families and promoting child wellbeing in Mono County. About the Coordinator Kevin Lian brings a countywide perspective to his work at the Mono County Office of Education, where he leads child and family initiatives focused on prevention, early learning, and student well-being. Drawing on years of experience managing cross-agency programs and partnerships, he helps align local efforts to better serve children and families in Mono County. Through the Child Abuse Prevention Council, Kevin works closely with community organizations, schools, and public agencies to strengthen collaboration, promote family resilience, and build awareness around child abuse prevention and support services. Report Child or Adult Abuse To report suspected child or adult abuse, call the 24-hour hotline at 1-800-340-5411. title: General Plan | Mono County California
content: General Plan | Mono County California Skip to main content Mono County California’s Eastern Sierra Search form Search Planning Division General Plan Government Code § 65300 requires each county to "adopt a comprehensive long-term general plan for the physical development of the county." Mono County is unique in that the General Plan and Zoning Code have been combined into one document. The purpose of the Mono County General Plan is to establish policies to guide decisions on future growth, development, and conservation of natural resources in the unincorporated area of the county. The plan reflects community-based planning and includes individual area plans for Mono County communities. GENERAL PLAN MAPS General Plan EIR Specific Plans Parcel Viewer Resource Documents The following summarizes the vision of the Mono County General Plan: The environmental and economic integrity of Mono County shall be maintained and enhanced through orderly growth, minimizing land use conflicts, supporting local tourist and agricultural based economies, and protecting the scenic, recreational, cultural, and natural resources of the area. The small-town atmosphere, rural- residential character and associated quality of life will be sustained consistent with community plans. Mono County will collaborate with applicable federal, state and local entities in pursuing this vision through citizen-based planning and efficient, coordinated permit processing. The planning areas of June Lake and Mono Basin also have separately published area or community plans, which are incorporated into the Land Use Element of the General Plan. These documents will be posted below when they are available. The work upon which this publication is based was funded in part through a grant awarded by the California Strategic Growth Council. Disclaimer: The statements and conclusions of this report are those of Mono County and not necessarily those of the California Strategic Growth Council or of the California Department of Conservation, or its employees. The California Strategic Growth Council and the California Department of Conservation make no warranties, express or implied, and assume no liability for the information contained in the succeeding text. * On January 31, 2023, the Board of Forestry and Fire Protection (BOF) approved comprehensive updates to the State's Minimum Fire Safe Regulations (MFSR). These newly approved regulations supersede Mono County’s regulations and noteworthy changes include 30' setback requirements for all parcels regardless of land use designation and parcel size (unless certain criteria can be met for deviation in Article 5 of those regulations). Emergency water standards required by the Mono County General Plan for Long Valley and Wheeler Crest Fire Protection Districts still remain in effect. Please use the online California Code of Regulations to refer to the most current version of the MFSR. title: Fictitious Business Name Statement (FBN) | Mono County California
content: Fictitious Business Name Statement (FBN) | Mono County California Skip to main content Mono County California’s Eastern Sierra Search form Search Clerk Recorder Fictitious Business Name Statement (FBN) Any person or entity who regularly transacts business, under a fictitious name, in Mono County is required to file a Fictitious Business Name (FBN) Statement with the County Clerk within 40 days of first transacting business. This requirement is for consumer protection and is mandated under Business and Professions Code Sections 17900-17930 and 14411 – 14418 , and Government Code Sections 6020 - 6027 , 6040 , and 6064 . Filing ensures the public has access to the true name and mailing address of the owners of a business ( B & P Code Section § 17920 ). A Fictitious Business Name Statement is a public record . California law requires FBNs to be filed at the County level; there is no Statewide or national registry. (If the registrant has no place of business within the State, the filing shall be with the Clerk of Mono County.) Who must file a Fictitious Business Name? You must file if: You are doing business for profit under a name that does not include your legal surname. The business name does not clearly indicate the nature of the business or every owner involved. A previous filing has changed (e.g., new ownership, change from sole proprietor to LLC, address changes, etc.). You must refile within 40 days of the change. You do not need to file if: The business name includes the full legal surname of the owner and does not imply additional owners. The business is a nonprofit organization. Examples: Sam Hunt Gardening → No FBN required Hunt Gardening → No FBN required Hunt and Company Gardening → FBN required (implies more owners) Corporations, LLCs, limited partnerships, and LLPs must file if the business name is anything other than the exact name registered with the Secretary of State. Overview Fees One registrant name or a married couple: $25.00 (ONE Business name) Additional Business names: $6.00 each Additional registrant names: $6.00 each Abandonment of FBN: $25.00 Certified copy of FBN: $2.00 Accepted Payment Methods: Cash - (in person only and must be exact) Check Money Order Credit Cards - (in person) Please make payments payable to Mono County Clerk. We do not accept FBN Statement filings via email. The County Clerk's Office does not issue Business Licenses. Contact the Mono County Treasurer-Tax Collector for more information. Resale licenses / seller’s permits are issued by the State Board of Equalization . Tax ID numbers are issued by the Franchise Tax Board . Requirements Application must be signed by the appropriate owner or officer* Corporate officer for corporations Manager/officer for LLCs General partner for partnerships Trustee for trusts Must list the street address of the principal place of business and county Corporations, LLCs, LPs, and LLPs must provide a Certificate of Status (within the last 6 months) & Articles of Incorporation/Organization issued by the Secretary of State. Screenshots are not accepted. All owners and their mailing addresses must be listed.

title: Fictitious Business Name Statement (FBN) | Mono County California
content: for corporations Manager/officer for LLCs General partner for partnerships Trustee for trusts Must list the street address of the principal place of business and county Corporations, LLCs, LPs, and LLPs must provide a Certificate of Status (within the last 6 months) & Articles of Incorporation/Organization issued by the Secretary of State. Screenshots are not accepted. All owners and their mailing addresses must be listed. Effective Jan 1, 2015: An Affidavit of Identity must accompany every FBN filing (new, renewal, or refiling). Tips Before Filing Research your business name to see if it’s available or to locate past filings. Email clerkrecorder@mono.ca.gov with possible name options; we can do a basic search and email results. Do not abbreviate names of people, businesses, or places. If a mistake is made after filing, the statement must be refiled, republished, and all fees must be repaid. Publishing Requirements After filing with the County Clerk: You must publish the FBN Statement in a newspaper of general circulation in Mono County. once a week for 4 consecutive weeks. Publication must begin within 45 days of filing. If publication does not occur within this timeframe, the FBN expires and must be re-filed with new fees. The newspaper will provide an Affidavit of Publication, which must be filed with the County Clerk within 45 days of the final publication date. Once these requirements are met, your FBN is valid for 5 years. Renewal Registrants are responsible for renewing their Fictitious Business Name (FBN) Statement within 40 days after the expiration date. All applications for FBN renewal statements must be submitted with the required fees. When Publication Is Not Required A Renewal FBN does not require publication if both of the following are true: The renewal is filed no more than 40 days after the expiration date; and The business name and registrants are exactly the same as the original filing. When Publication Is Required If the renewal does not meet either criterion above, it is treated as a new filing, and publication is required. Examples of changes that trigger publication: Hunt and Company Gardening → Hunt and Company Gardening, LLC Hunt and Company Gardening → Hunt and Co Gardening Hunt and Company Gardening → Hunt and Sons Gardening Hunt and Company Gardening → Hunt and Company Gardening and Landscaping physical or mailing address changes changes in registrant name or the number of registrants Withdrawal/Abandonment If you stop using a business name and the FBN was filed within the last 5 years, you must file a Statement of Abandonment . Abandonments must be filed and published in the same manner as the original FBN. File an FBN Statement in Person Complete the FBN Statement online. Bring the complete but unsigned application to the office. Bring a valid government-issued photo ID . Appointments are strongly recommended. Application for Fictitious Business Name Statement Location: Mono County Clerk 74 N. School Street Bridgeport, CA 93517 Call us at (760)932-5530 or email us at clerkrecorder@mono.ca.gov to schedule an appointment. File an FBN Statement by Mail Complete the FBN Statement application. Have the Affidavit of Identity notarized.
